title: Rust from Zero
description: A comprehensive introduction to the Rust programming language. Starting from installation and basic syntax, you will master ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, traits, generics, error handling, concurrency, and async programming. The course culminates in building a real CLI application that ties all concepts together.

modules.title: Setup and Hello World
modules.description: Install Rust via rustup, configure your editor, and write your first program. Understand cargo, crates, and the Rust compilation model.
objectives: Install Rust and configure the toolchain with rustup
Create a new project with cargo
Compile and run a Hello World program
Understand the role of Cargo.toml and Cargo.lock

modules.title: Variables, Types, and Mutability
modules.description: Learn about immutability by default, type inference, scalar types, compound types, and shadowing.
objectives: Declare immutable and mutable variables
Use scalar types: integers, floats, booleans, characters
Work with tuples and arrays
Explain shadowing and when to use it

modules.title: Control Flow and Functions
modules.description: Master if/else expressions, loops (loop, while, for), and function definitions with parameters and return types.
objectives: Write functions with typed parameters and return values
Use if/else as expressions
Iterate with for, while, and loop
Understand statements vs expressions in Rust

modules.title: Ownership and Move Semantics
modules.description: Rust's central concept: ownership rules, move semantics, and the Copy trait. Understand how Rust manages memory without a garbage collector.
objectives: State the three ownership rules
Explain move semantics and when values are moved vs copied
Identify which types implement Copy
Trace ownership through function calls

modules.title: References and Borrowing
modules.description: Borrow values with shared and mutable references. Understand the borrow checker rules and why they prevent data races at compile time.
objectives: Create shared (&T) and mutable (&mut T) references
Explain the borrowing rules enforced by the compiler
Fix common borrow checker errors
Understand the relationship between ownership and borrowing

modules.title: Structs and Methods
modules.description: Define custom data types with structs. Implement methods and associated functions using impl blocks.
objectives: Define structs with named, tuple, and unit variants
Implement methods with &self, &mut self, and self receivers
Write associated functions (constructors)
Use the derive macro for Debug and Display

modules.title: Enums and Pattern Matching
modules.description: Model complex data with enums, including variants with data. Use match, if let, and while let for exhaustive and concise pattern matching.
objectives: Define enums with unit, tuple, and struct variants
Use match expressions with exhaustive patterns
Apply if let and while let for concise matching
Understand Option<T> as a core Rust enum

modules.title: Error Handling with Result and Option
modules.description: Handle errors idiomatically using Result<T, E>, the ? operator, and custom error types. Understand the difference between recoverable and unrecoverable errors.
objectives: Use Result<T, E> for recoverable error handling
Propagate errors with the ? operator
Define custom error types with thiserror
Choose between panic!, unwrap, expect, and proper error handling

modules.title: Collections: Vec, String, HashMap
modules.description: Work with Rust's standard collections. Understand heap allocation, String vs &str, and iterator-based collection manipulation.
objectives: Create and manipulate Vec<T> with push, pop, and indexing
Explain the difference between String and &str
Use HashMap for key-value storage
Iterate over collections with for loops and iterators

modules.title: Traits and Trait Bounds
modules.description: Define shared behavior with traits. Implement traits for your types and use trait bounds to write generic, polymorphic code.
objectives: Define and implement traits
Use default method implementations
Apply trait bounds on generic functions
Understand common standard library traits (Display, Debug, Clone, From)

modules.title: Generics
modules.description: Write reusable code with generic type parameters on functions, structs, enums, and impl blocks. Understand monomorphization.
objectives: Define generic functions and structs
Apply multiple trait bounds with where clauses
Explain monomorphization and its performance implications
Use generics with Option, Result, and Vec

modules.title: Lifetimes
modules.description: Understand lifetime annotations and how they help the compiler verify that references are always valid. Learn lifetime elision rules and common patterns.
objectives: Explain why lifetime annotations are needed
Annotate function signatures with lifetime parameters
Apply lifetime annotations on structs that hold references
Understand the three lifetime elision rules

modules.title: Closures and Iterators
modules.description: Use closures for inline anonymous functions and master the iterator trait for functional-style data processing chains.
objectives: Write closures that capture variables from their environment
Understand Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce traits
Chain iterator adaptors: map, filter, fold, collect
Create custom iterators by implementing the Iterator trait

modules.title: Modules, Crates, and Cargo
modules.description: Organize code into modules and crates. Manage dependencies with Cargo, publish crates, and structure multi-file projects.
objectives: Structure code with mod, pub, and use
Organize a project into multiple files and directories
Add and manage external crate dependencies
Understand the difference between library and binary crates

modules.title: Smart Pointers: Box, Rc, RefCell
modules.description: Go beyond stack allocation with Box for heap data, Rc for shared ownership, and RefCell for interior mutability.
objectives: Use Box<T> for heap allocation and recursive types
Share ownership with Rc<T> and Arc<T>
Apply interior mutability with RefCell<T>
Identify when each smart pointer type is appropriate

modules.title: Concurrency with Threads
modules.description: Write fearless concurrent code with threads, message passing via channels, and shared state with Mutex and Arc.
objectives: Spawn threads with std::thread::spawn
Send data between threads using channels (mpsc)
Share mutable state safely with Arc<Mutex<T>>
Explain how Rust's type system prevents data races

modules.title: Async Programming with Tokio
modules.description: Write non-blocking asynchronous code with async/await syntax and the Tokio runtime. Handle concurrent I/O efficiently.
objectives: Understand the async/await model and Futures
Set up and use the Tokio async runtime
Perform concurrent I/O operations with async functions
Use tokio::select! and tokio::join! for task management

modules.title: Testing in Rust
modules.description: Write unit tests, integration tests, and doc tests using Rust's built-in test framework. Organize test code and use assertions effectively.
objectives: Write unit tests with #[test] and assert macros
Organize integration tests in the tests/ directory
Test error conditions with #[should_panic] and Result returns
Run tests selectively with cargo test filters

modules.title: Capstone: Build a CLI Application
modules.description: Apply everything you have learned to build a complete command-line tool using clap for argument parsing, serde for serialization, and proper error handling.
objectives: Design and structure a multi-module CLI application
Parse command-line arguments with clap
Read and write files with serde serialization
Implement comprehensive error handling and user-friendly output
